title: Roast duck with orange
subtitle: Whole roasted duck with a sharp orange and honey glaze.
excerpt: Whole duck scored and slow-roasted to render the fat, then glazed with orange juice, honey, and ginger in the final twenty minutes. The skin crisps. The meat stays juicy. A far more achievable centrepiece than duck's reputation suggests.
sourceNotes: Duck with orange is a French classic (canard à l'orange) that has been absorbed into British restaurant and home cooking since the mid-twentieth century. The French version uses a rich bigarade sauce made from bitter orange and duck stock; the British adaptation simplifies this to a glaze applied during roasting. Whole duck cookery requires patience with fat rendering: duck carries far more subcutaneous fat than chicken, and the cooking time reflects the need to render this out slowly before the final glaze.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Score the duck and dry it uncovered in the fridge overnight for crisper skin.

content.text: Duck needs longer than chicken to cook well, not because the meat is tougher, but because the fat layer under the skin must render. Rushing this produces pale, flabby skin sitting on a thick layer of soft fat. Scoring the skin and giving the bird two hours at moderate heat produces crispy skin with most of the fat underneath it gone.
The glaze goes on only in the last 20 minutes. Applied earlier, the honey burns before the fat has rendered.

items.prepNote: 1 juiced and zested, 1 halved for the cavity

content.text: A roasting tin with a rack if available, so the duck sits out of its own fat. A meat thermometer is useful.

Heat the oven to 190 °C fan. Score the skin of the duck in a crosshatch pattern, cutting through the skin and fat but not into the flesh. Season all over with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and {{black-pepper}} of black pepper. Push the halved orange into the cavity. Place breast-side up on a rack in a roasting tin. Roast for 2 hours, draining off the accumulated fat into a jar after the first hour (save it — it is excellent for roasting potatoes).

Mix the orange juice and zest, {{honey}} of honey, {{soy-sauce-dark}} of soy sauce, and {{ginger-root}} of grated ginger. Brush generously over the duck. Return to the oven for 20 minutes, brushing with more glaze halfway through, until the skin is deep mahogany and caramelised. Rest for 15 minutes before carving.

items.symptom: Skin is still pale and soft after two hours
items.cause: Duck was not scored deeply enough, or the fat layer was not draining.
items.fix: Drain the fat from the tin and increase the oven to 200 °C for the final 20 minutes before glazing.

items.symptom: Glaze is burning before the bird is cooked through
items.cause: Applied too early, or oven temperature too high.
items.fix: Cover the breast loosely with foil and continue cooking. Apply the glaze only in the final 20 minutes.

items.symptom: Meat is still pink inside after the full time
items.cause: Duck often looks slightly pink and is safe; check with a thermometer.
items.fix: Duck breast is safe at 63 °C (it may be slightly pink); legs need 75 °C. Use a thermometer rather than colour as the test.

items.symptom: Not enough glaze flavour on the skin
items.fix: Apply two or three coats over the last 20 minutes, letting each set before the next.

content.text: Duck with orange arrived in Britain through French restaurant influence and became a dinner party standard in the 1970s, when French cuisine set the benchmark for sophisticated home cooking. Canard à l'orange in its classic form requires a bigarade (bitter orange) sauce that is technically demanding; the glaze approach here delivers the same contrast of rich dark meat against sharp citrus in a format accessible without specialist ingredients. The saved duck fat, which fills a jar after two hours of rendering, is one of the better secondary yields of any recipe in this collection.
